en.extract:
The azure-naped jay (Cyanocorax heilprini) is a species of bird in the family Corvidae.
It is found in Brazil, Colombia, and Venezuela.
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical dry shrubland.


Taxonomy
The Azure-naped jay was first described by Alan F. Gentry based on a single specimen, marked as a male, from the T. B. Wilson Collection of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia.
The genus Cyanocorax comes from the Ancient Greek kuanos (dark blue) and korax (raven). The species epithet heilprini honours Gentry's friend, Professor Angelo Heilprin.
There are two identified subspecies:

C. h. heilprini. The nominate subspecies.
C. h. hafferi. This subspecies, Campina Jay, was described as a new species in 2013, but the South American Classification Committee of the American Ornithologists' Union did not support the proposal.


Description
The back is dark brown, as are the upper sides of the wings and tail. The rear crown and nape are bright lavender blue. The front and sides of the head are black, with the feathers of the forecrown and forehead curled up and forwards in a short bushy crest. From the chin to the centre of the breast the bird is dark violaceous-grey. The lower breast and belly are violaceous, fading to white on the lower belly and vent. The eyes are pale to yellowish-white and the bill, feet and legs are black.


Distribution and habitat
This species is a native resident species of the Amazon basin, found from Southeast Colombia to Southwest Venezuela (Amazonas) and extreme northwest Brazil.
It is a bird of the lower tropical zone, found at altitudes of 250m and below.  Its preferred habitat is stunted forests, forest edges and second growth on sandy soils in the upper Río Negro basin, as well as in lighter savannah woodland. Two recent surveys of birds Amazon basin found the Azure-naped jay is endemic to areas of white sand forest, and is not found in nearby areas of nearby terra firme forests, seasonally flooded forests and Amazonian savannas.


Behaviour
These birds are found in noisy groups that travel slowly, foraging at all levels. They are wary of observers and will utter alarm cries, taking turns to observe an intruder before moving on, or simply disappear into surrounding vegetation.


Status
The azure-naped jay is rated a species of Least Concern by Birdlife International, because it occurs over a very large range and the population, though declining, is not thought to be declining rapidly enough to reach the threshold of Vulnerable status.


References

The '''azure-naped jay''' ('''''Cyanocorax heilprini''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Corvidae]].
It is found in [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], and [[Venezuela]].

Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical dry [[shrubland]].

==Taxonomy==

The Azure-naped jay was first described by Alan F. Gentry based on a single specimen, marked as a male, from the T. B. Wilson Collection of the Academy of Natural Sciences of [[Philadelphia]].<ref name=Gentry>{{cite journal |last1=Gentry |first1=Alan F. |title=Description of a New Species of the Genus Cyanocorax |journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia |date=1885 |volume=37 |page=90 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/30490#page/90/mode/1up |access-date=24 January 2021 |publisher=Methuen & co., ltd.}}</ref>

The [[genus]] [[Cyanocorax]] comes from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''kuanos'' (dark blue) and ''korax'' (raven). The species epithet ''heilprini'' honours Gentry's friend, Professor [[Angelo Heilprin]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Jobling |first1=James A. |title=The Helm dictionary of scientific bird names: from aalge to zusii |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |page=126}}</ref>
There are two identified [[subspecies]]:

* ''C. h. heilprini''. The nominate subspecies.
* ''C. h. hafferi''. This subspecies, [[Campina Jay]], was described as a new species in 2013, but the [[South American Classification Committee]] of the [[American Ornithologists' Union]] did not support the proposal.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Cohn-Haft |first1=Mario |last2=dos Santos Junior |first2=Marcelo A. |last3=Fernandes |first3=Alexandre M. |last4=Ribas |first4=Camila C., in |title=Handbook of the birds of the world: New Species and Global Index |date=2013 |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona |isbn=978-84-96553-88-0 |pages=48–53 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/260034087 |access-date=24 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Part 9. Oscine Passeriformes, A (Vireonidae to Sturnidae) |title=A classification of the bird species of South America |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline09.htm |website=www.museum.lsu.edu |publisher=South American Classification Committee |access-date=24 January 2021}}</ref>

==Description==

The back is dark brown, as are the upper sides of the wings and tail. The rear crown and nape are bright lavender blue. The front and sides of the head are black, with the feathers of the forecrown and forehead curled up and forwards in a short bushy crest. From the chin to the centre of the breast the bird is dark violaceous-grey. The lower breast and belly are violaceous, fading to white on the lower belly and vent. The eyes are pale to yellowish-white and the bill, feet and legs are black.<ref name=Restall>{{cite book |last1=Restall |first1=Robin L. |last2=Rodner |first2=Clemencia |last3=Lentino |first3=Miguel |title=Birds of northern South America: an identification guide |date=2007 |publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven |isbn=978-0-300-10862-0 |pages=576–577}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

This species is a native resident species of the Amazon basin, found from Southeast Colombia to Southwest Venezuela (Amazonas) and extreme northwest Brazil.<ref name=Birdlife>{{cite web |title=Species factsheet: Cyanocorax heilprini |url=http://www.birdlife.org |website=BirdLife International (2021) |access-date=24 January 2021}}</ref>

It is a bird of the lower tropical zone, found at altitudes of 250m and below.  Its preferred habitat is stunted forests, forest edges and second growth on sandy soils in the upper [[Rio Negro (Amazon)|Río Negro]] basin, as well as in lighter savannah woodland.<ref name="Restall" /> Two recent surveys of birds Amazon basin found the Azure-naped jay is endemic to areas of [[Sand forest|white sand forest]], and is not found in nearby areas of nearby terra firme forests, seasonally flooded forests and Amazonian savannas.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borges |first1=Sérgio Henrique |last2=Whittaker |first2=Andrew |last3=Almeida |first3=Ricardo Afonso Machado de |title=Bird diversity in the Serra do Aracá region, northwestern Brazilian Amazon: preliminary check-list with considerations on biogeography and conservation |journal=Zoologia (Curitiba) |date=August 2014 |volume=31 |issue=4 |page=358 |doi=10.1590/S1984-46702014000400006 |issn=1984-4670|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borges |first1=Sérgio Henrique |last2=Cornelius |first2=Cintia |last3=Ribas |first3=Camila |last4=Almeida |first4=Ricardo |last5=Guilherme |first5=Edson |last6=Aleixo |first6=Alexandre |last7=Dantas |first7=Sidnei |last8=Santos |first8=Marcos Pérsio Dos |last9=Moreira |first9=Marcelo |title=What is the avifauna of Amazonian white-sand vegetation? |journal=Bird Conservation International |date=June 2016 |volume=26 |issue=2 |page=6 |doi=10.1017/S0959270915000052|s2cid=88411073 }}</ref>

==Behaviour==

These birds are found in noisy groups that travel slowly, foraging at all levels. They are wary of observers and will utter alarm cries, taking turns to observe an intruder before moving on, or simply disappear into surrounding vegetation.<ref name="Restall" />

==Status==

The azure-naped jay is rated a species of Least Concern by Birdlife International, because it occurs over a very large range and the population, though declining, is not thought to be declining rapidly enough to reach the threshold of Vulnerable status.<ref name="Birdlife" />

zh.extract:
蓝枕蓝鸦（学名：Cyanocorax heilprini），是鸦科蓝鸦属的一种，分布于巴西、委内瑞拉和哥伦比亚。全球活动范围约为141,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
蓝枕蓝鸦的栖息地包括亚热带或热带的旱林、亚热带或热带的湿润低地林、干燥的稀树草原、亚热带或热带严重退化的前森林和亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛。


参考文献

Cyanocorax heilprini. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
